coredata.dc:title: Phosphor doped carbon dots with high photoluminescence and stability towards pH and Cr(VI) sensors
coredata.dc:creator: Tian, Yi
Yue, Yunlong
coredata.dc:description: To improve the photoluminescence (PL) and increase photostability of carbon dots (CDs), phosphorus doped CDs with bright green emitting were prepared using (NH4)2HPO4 and m-phenylenediamine (MPD), and terephthalic acid (TA) via a solvothermal synthesis. Compared to undoped CDs (M−CDs), the PL efficiency of P-doped sample (M−6 %P-CDs) created using optimized conditions was improved from 55.1 to 73.9 %. These bright CDs were incorporated in films to test the stability, in which the photostability in liquid and polymer films were improved by 6 and 37 %, respectively. Namely, it is found that P affected the functional groups of on the surface and inside CDs. Phosphorus promoted the dehydration condensation reaction of precursors, increased the structural defects of the CDs, separated the sp2 structural domains, and retained more oxygen-containing functional groups. In the case of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) instead of MPD, the PL efficiency of P-doped CDs was decreased. This is ascribed to P impeding the polymerization process of OPD. These CDs were used for pH and Cr(VI) ion sensors. P-doping improved the sensitivity of CDs to pH and Cr(VI). The pKa value of sample M−6 %P-CDs was 8.32 which was much lower compared with undoped one (∼1.97). Moreover, P-doped CDs revealed significantly enhanced sensitivity response to Cr(VI). The detection range and detection limit of M−6 %P-CDs for Cr(VI) was 1–130 μM and 0.59 μM, respectively. These results are crucial for the preparation and applications of CDs.

head.ce:title: Phosphor doped carbon dots with high photoluminescence and stability towards pH and Cr(VI) sensors

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material Science and Engineering, University of Jinan, Jinan 250022, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To improve the photoluminescence (PL) and increase photostability of carbon dots (CDs), phosphorus doped CDs with bright green emitting were prepared using (NH4)2HPO4 and m-phenylenediamine (MPD), and terephthalic acid (TA) via a solvothermal synthesis. Compared to undoped CDs (M−CDs), the PL efficiency of P-doped sample (M−6 %P-CDs) created using optimized conditions was improved from 55.1 to 73.9 %. These bright CDs were incorporated in films to test the stability, in which the photostability in liquid and polymer films were improved by 6 and 37 %, respectively. Namely, it is found that P affected the functional groups of on the surface and inside CDs. Phosphorus promoted the dehydration condensation reaction of precursors, increased the structural defects of the CDs, separated the sp2 structural domains, and retained more oxygen-containing functional groups. In the case of o-phenylenediamine (OPD) instead of MPD, the PL efficiency of P-doped CDs was decreased. This is ascribed to P impeding the polymerization process of OPD. These CDs were used for pH and Cr(VI) ion sensors. P-doping improved the sensitivity of CDs to pH and Cr(VI). The pKa value of sample M−6 %P-CDs was 8.32 which was much lower compared with undoped one (∼1.97). Moreover, P-doped CDs revealed significantly enhanced sensitivity response to Cr(VI). The detection range and detection limit of M−6 %P-CDs for Cr(VI) was 1–130 μM and 0.59 μM, respectively. These results are crucial for the preparation and applications of CDs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) as a zero-dimensional nanomaterial by carbon as the basic constituent are tending instead of traditional fluorescent probes or semiconductor quantum dots due to their excellent photoluminescence (PL) properties, easily modifiable surfaces, cheap and non-toxicity, and potential applications in energy, catalysis and biomedicine [1–4]. Although CDs exhibit advantages in various fields, challenges such as low PL quantum yields (PLQYs) and limited photostability persist [5,6]. This is partly due to the fact that the CDs themselves have many defects that are difficult to control, such as surface states, dislocations, vacancies, etc., thus increasing the non-radiative complexation of CDs; on the other hand, the current synthesis methods still lack the precise control of the structure and surface chemistry of CDs [5,7]. Both under- and over-carbonization lead to a decrease in the PLQYs of CDs, while surface functional groups can both passivate surface defects and enhance fluorescence emission, as well as tend to form non-radiative states and destroy the emission center [8]. Unlike the PLQYs, which are the core issue of common concern, the photostability of CDs is an often overlooked but very important property. Although early reports suggested that CDs have excellent photostability, a large number of subsequent studies have shown that CDs suffer from photobleaching [9]. The major challenge lies in the incompatibility between the PLQYs of CDs and their photostability, which severely hampers the advancement of CDs [10]. This is because it is generally believed that high PLQYs CDs have a higher contribution of fluorescent molecular groups in their luminous centers, and most of these molecular fluorophores do not have the protection of graphite lattice, and thus the molecular fluorophores are highly susceptible to photodecomposition or oxidation under ultraviolet radiation [7,10,11].

To address the above issues, it is found that strategies such as surface passivation, matrix-assisted and elemental doping to enhance their PL properties and stability [2,12]. Among these, elemental doping can alter the structural and electronic properties of CDs, which is a relatively effective and low-cost method. Among the various dopants, nitrogen atom is the most commonly used dopant because it is similar to carbon in electron structure and size [13]. Nitrogen can replace carbon atoms into the carbon skeleton, and its lone pair of electrons has a strong affinity for sp2 carbon domains, which can act as π-bonds to enhance electron transfer in the carbon nucleus. It has been suggested that the increase of graphitic and pyrrolic nitrogen can significantly enhance the PLQY of CDs [14]. Boron atoms also have a similar radius to carbon and can usually enter the carbon backbone in the form of sp2 hybridization, but do not provide as much electron transfer as nitrogen because the 2pz orbitals of boron atoms are empty after hybridization. The radius of the sulfur atom is much larger than that of the carbon atom, so that it is usually difficult to dope into the carbon backbone, but rather modifies the edge sites of the carbon. It mainly regulates the PLQY of CDs in the form of energy trap, but it is difficult to affect the conjugated structure [15]. Moreover, sulfur's electronegativity (2.58) is close to that of carbon (2.55), making it less capable of regulating electron density [14].

Phosphorus has the same number of valence electrons as nitrogen, and both elements tend to exhibit similar chemical properties and can act as n-type donors to form substitution deficiencies in carbon clusters [16]. Moreover, phosphorus has a larger atomic radius and higher electron donating capacity than nitrogen [16]. The P-C bond is longer than the N-C bond, and when phosphorus replaces carbon into the carbon skeleton or enters the carbon interlayer, it will bring greater changes to the crystal plane spacing and electron density than nitrogen, thus affecting the lattice distortion [17,18]. In addition, phosphorus exists in carbon materials mainly in the form of oxygen-containing functional groups, which can enhance the hydrophilicity of CDs and significantly broaden the application of CDs in water detection [15,19]. Therefore, phosphorus is often co-doped with nitrogen in the CDs [20]. Zhou et al. prepared blue-emitting CDs using phosphorus tribromide as the phosphorus source used for cell imaging. They found that P-doping can increase the PLQYs from 3.4 % to 25 %, which may be due to the introduction of more sp2 clusters or defects by the phosphorus in the CDs [21]. Kumar et al. demonstrated that the composite structure of phosphorus-doped carbon nitride-based CDs with TiO2 enhances charge separation and electron transfer, thereby significantly improving photocatalytic efficiency [20]. Early phosphor sources were mostly inorganic phosphoric acid [22,23], later researchers found that using organic phosphoric acid as the phosphorus source could improve the N/P doping of CDs [19,24]. However, the use of organophosphorus source is not only expensive but also cannot avoid the problem of corrosion of equipment caused by liquid acid and low PLQYs of CDs. Therefore, Xu et al. prepared blue-emitting CDs with a PLQYs of 53.8 % using (NH4)2HPO4 as the phosphorus source for mercury ion detection [25]. However, most current studies have not systematically studied the mechanism of P-doping, and mostly ignored the impact of phosphorus on the photostability of CDs. Therefore, further research is needed to elucidate the mechanism behind P-doping.

Because of high sensitivity, selectivity, stability, and biosafety, CDs can be used for detecting solution pH value, heavy metal ions, and antibiotics in water [26,27]. Among these areas of study, pH value plays a crucial role in environmental, chemical, and biological sciences. Additionally, Cr(VI) is an extremely toxic pollutant that poses risks to both water quality and biosafety in industrial wastewater [28]. Therefore, exploring methods to control the structure of CDs for the preparation of highly efficient pH value and Cr(VI) sensors represents a valuable research topic. However, CDs still suffer from drawbacks including low PLQYs and poor solubility in water. Moreover, the adjustment mechanism of CDs modification methods on the pH sensitivity remains unclear; thus accurate control over its pH detection range proves challenging [29]. Moreover, the selectivity and detection sensitivity of CDs towards Cr(VI) also require improvement to achieve precise detection in complex aquatic environments [30,31].

In this paper, (NH4)2HPO4 was used as a phosphorus source for producing CDs with high PLQYs towards pH and Cr (VI) sensitive. (NH4)2HPO4 is an inexpensive and easily obtainable compound that does not corrode equipment. The addition of P-doping enhances the PLQYs of M−6 %-P-CDs, synthesized using m-phenylenediamine (MPD) and terephthalic acid (TA) as carbon sources, from 55.1 to 73.9 %, without affecting the emission wavelength. Characterization and control experiments reveal that (NH4)2HPO4 promotes the dehydration condensation reaction of MPD derivatives while hindering electrophilic substitution reactions, resulting in more isolated sp2 domains. M−6 %P-CDs can serve as an acid-based fluorescent indicator and exhibit strong selective quenching towards the important heavy metal pollutant Cr (VI) in water, with a detection limit of 0.70 μM. Furthermore, P-doping enhances the photostability of CDs in solution by 6 % and in polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) films by 37 %. This research offers a valuable resource for the investigation of P-doped CDs.

MPD (99.5 %), o-phenylenediamine (OPD, 99.0 %), (NH4)2HPO4 (99.9 %), ethanol (99.5 %), NaOH (96.0 %), AlCl3·6H2O (97.0 %), PbCl2 (99.5 %), MnCl2·4H2O (99.0 %), MgCl2·6H2O (98.0 %), NiCl2·6H2O (99.0 %), FeCl3·6H2O (99.9 %), FeSO4·7H2O (99.99 %), ZnCl2 (98 %), CuCl2 (99.99 %), and K2Cr2O7 (99.5 %) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. The TA (99 %), CdCl2·5/2H2O (98 %) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. CoCl2·6H2O (99.0 %) and PVA (MW = 44.05) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. The HCl (36.0–38.0 w/%) was obtained from Yantai Far Eastern Fine Chemical Co., Ltd. Distilled water was used as a solvent to test the pH and ion sensitivity of CDs from a Millipore system (Merck 18.2 MΩ cm).

In a typical M−6 %P-CDs preparation process, 0.062 g MPD and 0.031 g TA as carbon precursors were dispersed in 10 mL ethanol. 0.032 g (NH4)2HPO4 was dissolved in 0.5 mL water, then the (NH4)2HPO4 solution was mixed with ethanol solution containing the precursors. After ultrasonic for 10 min, it was transferred to a 30 mL polytetrafluoroethylene reactor and heated at 180℃ for 12 h. After the reaction solution was cooled to room temperature, a 0.22 μm organic filter membrane was used to filter and remove large particles of impurities. The step of removing unreacted small molecules that may be present in the CDs was carried out after the above operation. A 1000 Da dialysis bag was used to dialysis in deionized water for 48 h (water changed every 8 h). Then the clear CDs solution was freeze-dried, diluted with ethanol and stored at room temperature for later use (12.5 mg/mL). The synthesis methods of other P-doped CDs are similar. The doping amount of phosphorus was calculated according to the percentage of phosphorus in the precursor to the total mass (0.125 g), and the ratio of MPD and TA is maintained at 2:1. M−CDs synthesized by dispersing 0.083 g MPD and 0.042 g TA in 10 mL ethanol solution, with the remaining steps consistent with other CDs.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI Talos F200S, US) was employed to examine the morphology and dimensions of M−6 %P-CDs. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were analyzed by a VG Micro Tech ESCA 300 infrared spectrophotometer (Thermo Electron, the USA). The Raman spectra of CDs were recorded by the Raman spectrometer (Lab RAM HR Evolution) with 532 nm wavelength excitation laser. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured by D8-ADVANCE (Germany) and Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (the USA), respectively. The excitation and emission spectra were analyzed by fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi F-4600, Japan). The ultraviolet (UV) absorption spectra and fluorescence decay plots were obtained by UV–vis spectrophotometer (Hitachi U-4100, Japan) and Jobin Yvon IBH fluorescence lifetime analyzer (Horiba, Japan), respectively. The PLQYs of CDs were obtained by reference sample Rhodamine 6G (QY = 98 % in ethanol). The pH values were tested by a calibrated acidometer (Hangzhou Qiwei Instrument Co., LTD. PHB-1). The radiative recombination rates (Гr) and non-radiative recombination rates (Гnr) were estimated as Eq. (1) and (2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cds solutions with different pH values and the calculation of pKa
ce:section.ce:para:
NaOH and HCl solution were used to adjust the pH of water. CDs solution was added to 2 mL water with desired pH value for the test (3.125 × 10-4 mg/mL). The relationship between pH and PL intensity can be expressed by Eq. (3).

are the PL intensity of all protonated systems and all deprotonated systems, respectively. The pKa is the pH value when the degree of protonation of the CDs is 50 %.

For selective detection of metal ions by CDs, 1 mM aqueous solutions of metal ions were mixed with CDs solution (3.125 × 10-4 mg/mL) within 2 min for the fluorescence test. The detection limit of chromium ions was calculated according to the formula 3σ/S, where σ is the standard deviation of the blank signal and S is the slope of the fitted line. The PL intensity of CDs versus ion concentration was fitted by the Stern-Volmer equation (Eq. (4)):

where, F0 is the initial PL intensity of M−6 %P-CDs in water, F is the PL intensity of M−6 %P-CDs in the presence of chromium ions. KSV is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant (slope of the concentration-dependent response).

Tap water and lake water samples were taken from the tap water pipe and Jiazi Lake on the campus of Jinan University, respectively, and the samples were taken directly and practically without treatment. Honey was purchased from local beekeepers, diluted with warm boiled water, and the concentration of the honey solution was 5 mg/mL.

To prepare CDs/PVA film, a solution of 0.2 g PVA in 10 mL water was heated and stirred for 15 min, along with the addition of 0.2 mL CDs solution of 12.5 mg/mL. That is, concentration of CDs in the PVA solution was 0.25 mg/mL. The CDs/PVA solution is evenly coated on the clean glass sheet, and the cured CDs/PVA film is removed from the glass sheet after standing for 1 day to dry.

The photo-stability tests were measured by placing the CDs ethanol solution (absorption value at 483 nm were controlled at 0.04) or CDs/polymer films under a portable handheld UV lamp at 365 nm. The samples were placed at a distance of 5 cm from the light source, and the change of their PL spectra were measured at intervals.

Scheme 1 illustrates the synthesis procedure of CDs. It is worth mentioning that, in order to remove the unreacted small molecules that may remain in the reaction, we explored the effect of the dialysis step on the fluorescence properties of the CDs. Experiments revealed that there were no significant changes in the fluorescence, absorption, PLQYs, and lifetime decay curves of the CDs before and after dialysis, which proved that the CDs had been sufficiently reacted, and that the residual small molecules have little effect on the PL properties of CDs. Therefore, this purification step was omitted considering the efficiency of the experiment.

Green-emitting M−CDs can be prepared by a one-step solvothermal method using MPD and TA as precursors with a PLQY of 55.1 %. In the presence of ethanol, the precursors undergo esterification, polymerization, crosslinking and dehydration to form CDs with compact sp2 carbon skeleton and abundant surface functional groups. To further enhance the PLQYs, (NH4)2HPO4 was introduced into the precursor and 0.5 mL of water was introduced into the solvent to assist its dissolution, and then phosphorus-doped M−6 %P-CDs were prepared under the same heating temperature and time. (NH4)2HPO4 reacted with alcohols to form phosphate esters that give more oxygen-containing functional groups to the surface of the CDs, while phosphorus also promoted the polymerization process of the precursor. (NH4)2HPO4 also released ammonia through thermal decomposition. Nitrogen entered the carbon core structure during the high-pressure thermal polymerization process and replaced some of the carbon elements. After phosphorus doping, the emission wavelength of the CDs remained unchanged, but their PLQYs were enhanced to 73.9 %. This demonstrated that phosphorus doping can effectively improve the PL properties of CDs.

This is due to the fact that phosphorus affects the internal carbon core as well as the external functional groups of CDs during their formation. First, phosphorus hindered the oriented alignment of the aromatic skeleton of the CDs precursor, and strong interactions between the P-containing lattice and the carbon lattice led to the disruption of the aligned structural domains of the carbon core [32]. Moreover, the insertion of phosphate groups into the organic structure induced structural expansion, triggering the deformation of the carbon core and splitting into small sp2 clusters. The abundant small sp2 clusters were accompanied by more edge defects and enhanced the active sites for binding molecular fluorophores, and also served as a suitable carbon substrate for better binding and protection of molecular fluorophores, which reduced the rate of nonradiative complexation of the CDs [8]. Moreover, electrons were absorbed by P atoms as lone-pair electron pairs, which facilitated n-π* leaps. In addition, the process of solvothermal transforms the phosphorus-containing functional groups in the carbon lattice from the unstable C-O-P and C3-P bonds to the more stable C-P-O and C3-P = O bonds [33].

Fig. 1 show the TEM images of sample M−6 %P-CDs, in which the sample revealed a spherical morphology with an average particle size of 3.2 ± 0.018 nm. M−6 %P-CDs revealed well-defined lattice stripes with lattice spacings of 0.18 and 0.21 nm, which correspond to the (004) and (100) crystal planes of the graphitic carbon (Fig. 1f–g) [34,35]. Interestingly, the graphitic phase structure of M−6 %P-CDs is not aligned in the same direction but produces distortions in different directions, as evidenced by Fig. 1g and h, demonstrating that the carbon core consists of several sp2 structural domains in a segregated manner rather than a single large sp2 structure.

Fig. 2 a shows the FTIR spectra of CDs with different P-doping levels. The peak at 3440 cm−1 originates from the stretching vibration of the N-H or O-H bond [36]. The peak at 1722 cm−1 originates from the polyimide group, whereas the undoped CDs do not contain any peaks of imine groups, suggesting that the introduction of (NH4)2HPO4 makes the CDs contain more thermally stable imine groups [37]. The peaks at 1500 and 1550 cm−1 correspond to the amide bond [38]. Among the M−6 %P-CDs, the C-N bond (1400 cm−1) [39], P = O (1274 cm−1) [22], P-O (1049 cm−1) [40], and P-N bond (958 cm−1) have the lowest intensities [23,41], while the peaks at 1620, 1550, and 1500 cm−1 corresponding to C = O and amide bonds have the relatively highest contents [38,39,42], suggesting that the M−6 %P-CDs contain more amide bonds as well as fewer surface defects. By comparing the FTIR spectra of CDs with different amounts of P-doping, it indicated that trace amounts of P-doping could significantly enhance the amide content in the CDs, but too much (NH4)2HPO4 reduces the amide bonding content, which can be attributed to the reduction of carbon content in the precursor. The introduction of a small amount of P atom means that more amino or hydroxyl groups can be introduced into the CDs (786 cm−1) [42,43], suggesting that P-doping may have weakened the carbonization of the CDs, thus retaining more surface functional groups. The corresponding peaks of P-O-P at 875 and 732 cm−1 prove the successful introduction of elemental P [23]. With the increase of P-doping, the intensity of the peaks of P-O-P and NH/OH gradually weakened, which proved that the structure of the CDs changed and the amino groups on their surfaces combined with P elements to generate P-N and P-O bonds.

Fig. 2b shows the Raman spectra of M−CDs and M−6 %P-CDs, where the D band at 1361 cm−1 and the G band at 1588 cm−1 represent the disordered and graphitic structure of the CDs, respectively. The ratio of the D band for the M−6 %P-CDs was 0.98, higher than that of the M−CDs which was 0.84, suggesting that P-doping increased the disordered structure of the CDs, which was due to the introduction of the heteroatom P increases the asymmetry in the precursor, which increases the separability of the sp2 structural domains in the lattice structure of the CDs [44].

Fig. 2c illustrates the major elemental composition and content of M−CDs and M−6 %P-CDs. It is evident that the doping of (NH4)2HPO4 in the precursor enhances the N content of the synthesized CDs, while the elemental P content (2.57 %) decreases compared to that in the precursor (6.00 %), with a doping efficiency of 42.8 %. The C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2d) of the M−CDs can be reverse-convoluted to 284.8 eV for C-C or C = C, 286.2 eV for C-O or C-N and 288.9 eV peaks for O-C = O [45,46]. The peak at 286.0 eV of M−6 %P-CDs is significantly enhanced, suggesting that the C-P binding energy is also included [18]. The fine spectra of the N element of CDs can be convolved into pyridinic nitrogen (399.2 eV), pyrrolic nitrogen (399.8 eV) and graphitic nitrogen (400.8 eV) (Fig. 2e) [47,48]. Compared with M−CDs, the peak intensities of pyrrolic nitrogen and graphitic nitrogen in M−6 %P-CDs were both increased and shifted 0.6 and 0.7 eV towards higher binding energies, demonstrating that the nitrogen atoms in M−6 %P-CDs tended to assemble into tighter core structures rather than form surface functional groups or defects. The O 1 s spectrum shows three peaks at 533.5 eV, 532.4 eV, 531.0 eV, which are assigned to C-O/ P-O, C = O, and O-H/P = O bonds, respectively (Fig. 2f) [23,49]. The significant enhancement of the peak at 531.0 eV proves that P-doping significantly alters the chemical environment of oxygen atoms. The peak for C = O is shifted by 0.3 eV towards higher binding energy, proving that electrons from oxygen atoms in the CDs are shifted towards phosphorus atoms. The fine scanning P 2p spectrum (Fig. 2g) from the M−6 %P-CDs surface consists of two major peaks located at 133.2 and 134.2 eV that can be ascribed to P-O/P = O and P-N [23]. This proves the successful introduction of the P atoms. The structural changes of CDs before and after doping with (NH4)2HPO4 are illustrated in Fig. 2h. Sample M−CDs have a complete sp2 carbon network structure of the carbon core, whereas after doping with (NH4)2HPO4, the pyrrolic nitrogen and graphitic nitrogen compositions are increased, and P atoms are inserted into the carbon core in the form of covalent bonding (e.g., −C-P = O bonding), which transform the sp2 carbon into sp3 carbon. As a result, the large sp2 network is partitioned into smaller portions to jointly form the carbon core.

Fig. 3 a shows the PL spectra of CDs with different amount of P-doping in the precursor. Interestingly, we found that P-doping does not change the emission wavelength of CDs, but can significantly enhance the PL intensity of CDs. The PL intensity of CDs tended to increase and then decrease with the increasing P content in precursors. The highest PLQY of the synthesized CDs was obtained when the P-doping in the precursor was 6 % (M−6 %P-CDs). The PLQY of M−6 %P-CDs was elevated from 55.1 to 73.9 % compared with that of M−CDs, which was partly attributed to the fact that the P-doping caused the CDs to contain more sp3 carbon constituents, and thus could produce stronger PL intensity than isolated sp2 carbon clusters [21,50]. To further investigate the regulation mechanism of P-doping on the PLQY of CDs, the absorption spectra of CDs with different P-doping amounts were investigated (Fig. 3b). The P-doping significantly enhanced the n-σ* leaps of amino or hydroxyl groups at 211 nm and the n-π* leaps of the C = O or P = O bonds at 291 nm [51], which further confirmed that P-doping could increase the surfaces of the CDs in terms of the amide, hydroxyl, and carboxyl group functional groups. This may be due to the fact that (NH4)2HPO4 provides a richer source of amino groups and can react with ethanol to form phosphate esters. The π-π* leaps of the C = C bond at 240 nm gradually decreased with the increase of P-doping, which is consistent with the trend of decreasing the proportion of the graphite phase of the P-doped CDs in Raman spectra [52]. The absorption peak at 460 nm is related to the strong emission, which corresponds to the surface state trapping excited state energy [53].

To investigate the reason for P-doping to enhance the PL intensity of the CDs, the fluorescence decay plots of the CDs with different P-doping in the precursor were further tested and their radiative and non-radiative loading rates were calculated (Fig. 3c, Table 1 ). The fluorescence decay plots conformed to a triple-exponential fit. There is still controversy about what the fitted values represent, but most scholars believe that the short-lifetime component τ1, the medium-lifetime component τ2, and the long-lifetime component τ3 can be attributed to the carbon core with a compact structure, the oxidized surface state, and the molecular state, respectively [54]. The fact that P-doping prolonged the average lifetimes was achieved by an increase in all three lifetimes. It indicates that P-doping modulates the electronic structure of the CDs, which facilitates charge separation and reduces the carrier complexation rate [18]. Interestingly, the average lifetimes of the CDs were positively correlated with their PLQYs, suggesting that P-doping can effectively passivate the surface defects of the CDs. The M−6 %P-CDs with the highest PLQY have both the lowest radiative (64.71 μs−1) and non-radiative (22.85 μs−1) complexation rates. This can be attributed to the fact that P-doped CDs inhibit the non-radiative leaps of π-π stacking of 2D carbon nanosheets by increasing the separability of the sp2 structural domains, which enhances the separation of photogenerated electrons and holes, and fills the defects and vacancies of the CDs [44].

Considering that the solvent control strategy can change the PL properties of CDs, the influence of the participation of water in the synthesis system was studied to further improve the synthesis process. The results are shown in Fig. 3d. The introduction of water in the solvent basically has no effect on the emission wavelength and waveform of the CDs, but significantly changes their PL intensity. The introduction of trace amount of water promoted the dissolution of (NH4)2HPO4, which promoted the homogeneous polymerization of the CDs. The 5 % water in the system can enhance the PL intensity by about double. However, excess water hindered the dehydration of the CDs.

Subsequently, in order to further explore the potential application of CDs, flexible fluorescent films were prepared from CDs and PVA, with results shown in Fig. 3e. The M−6 %P-CDs films were more transparent in daylight, while the M−CDs films were yellowish-brown in color. Under UV light irradiation, the fluorescent color of the M−CDs film was whitish, while the M−6 %P-CDs film emitted bright blue light. This proves the better PL performance of M−6 %P-CDs film, which is expected to be further applied in anti-counterfeiting, LED lighting and other fields.

Despite numerous characterizations, the underlying reason for P-doping to enhance the PLQY of CDs is still unclear, which is due to the fact that P atom modulates CDs in multiple ways; (i) P atoms have a larger radius compared to C atoms and thus can change the crystal plane spacing by substituting carbon atoms or carbon interlayer insertion, which lead to lattice distortion and reduce PL quenching due to π-π stacking [18]; (ii), P atoms can interact with defects in CDs and unpaired electrons in the dangling bonds of surface groups to form new electronic states [18]; (iii), P atoms with strong electron-donating ability can enhance the electron cloud density of the aromatic ring when introduced to the benzene ring of MPD, which in turn enhance the subsequent reaction rate. In order to investigate which factor plays a dominant role in the modulation of the PLQY, MPD in the precursor was replaced by OPD and named the samples as O-CDs to study the effect of the introduction of (NH4)2HPO4 on the synthesized process, and the results are shown in Fig. 3f. Surprisingly, unlike MPD, (NH4)2HPO4 does not enhance the PLQY of O-CDs, and the more (NH4)2HPO4, the lower the PL intensity of O-CDs. This is because the polymerization mechanism of the two different precursors is different when forming CDs [55]. The OPD forms dimers through electrophilic substitution reactions, which in turn carbonize and polymerize to form CDs [56,57]. In contrast, in the MPD system, the interstitial site of the amino-directed electrophilic substitution is occupied by another amino group, and thus the MPDs form methylquinoline amines structures mainly by dehydration condensation with crotonaldehyde, an oxidation product of ethanol [55,58]. First, the weakly basic environment created by (NH4)2HPO4 makes it more difficult to protonate the two amino groups of OPD, thus increasing the difficulty of its polymerization. In contrast, the synthesis of the fluorophore by MPD requires protonation of only one amino group, thus making the reaction easier to carry out compared to OPD as a precursor. Second, the strong electron-donating capacity of phosphorus may interfere with the electrophilic substitution reaction of OPD, thus preventing it from forming a fluorescent center. Finally, the phosphorus atom has a strong affinity for the oxygen atom in ethanol or crotonaldehyde, and the strong electron-donating ability of phosphorus atoms may weaken the C-O or C = O bond, which in turn facilitates the C-C bond coupling between MPD and crotonaldehyde [59]. In summary, factor (iii) plays a major role in regulating CDs. The (NH4)2HPO4 can enhance the PLQY of CDs with MPD as the precursor while weakening that with OPD as the precursors.

Subsequently, we explored the responsiveness of M−6 %P-CDs with high PLQY to pH in aqueous solution, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . Fig. 4a shows the PL spectra of M−6 %P-CDs in different pH environments. The acid-base difference significantly affects the intensity of the PL spectra, while the emission wavelength remains essentially unchanged. The PL intensity of M−6 %P-CDs is very low at pH = 1, which can be attributed to the aggregation of CDs due to the strong acidic environment [60]. The PL properties of the CDs remained stable when the pH = 2 ∼ 7, while their pH intensity decreased significantly when the pH was above 7. From the excitation spectra, the optimum excitation wavelength of the CDs at an emission wavelength of 514 nm under alkaline conditions showed a significant blue-shift from 450 to 398 nm, which proved that the alkaline environment significantly changes the structure of the CDs, which in turn affects their PL properties. This suggested that deprotonation of the surface of M−6 %P-CDs disrupted the charge balance of their photoluminescent centers, and thus required higher energy to excite their electron leaps. Fig. 4b clearly shows the changes of the PL intensity of the CDs at different pH values, which proves that the alkaline environment has strong quenching effects on M−6 %P-CDs. Fig. 4c shows the fitted plots for calculating the pKa of M−6 %P-CDs and M−CDs. The pKa of CDs was 10.29 ± 0.04 before P-doping, and after P-doping, it decreased to 8.32 ± 0.08, which proved that P-doping enhanced the basicity sensitivity of CDs. This is due to the surface of the M−6 %P-CDs retains more functional groups such as carboxyl, phenol or hydroxyl groups, and the intermolecular hydrogen bonding effect stabilizes the carboxylate anion [61]. Fig. 4d shows photograph of M−6 %P-CDs in aqueous solutions of different pH values, revealing that the solution is transparent and slightly yellowish under acidic conditions, whereas the solution is colorless and transparent under alkaline conditions. The PL intensity of M−6 %P-CDs in alkaline solutions was significantly reduced under UV light.

To investigate the selective response of M−6 %P-CDs to mental ion, PL spectra of CDs dispersed in different metal solvents of 1 mM were tested (Fig. 5 a). Compared with M−CDs, M−6 %P-CDs has stronger selective quenching ability for Cr(VI), and can maintain its quenching effect under the condition of coexistence of other ions. The PL spectra of M−6 %P-CDs added with different concentrations of Cr(VI) were recorded, as shown in Fig. 5b and 5c. The PL intensity of M−6 %P-CDs decreased with the increase of Cr(VI) concentration. There is a good linear relationship between Cr(VI) concentration and the PL intensity of M−6 %P-CDs in the range of 1–130 μM (R2 = 0.999), and the detection limit is 0.59 μM. This result is significantly superior to those reported in the literature (Table 2 ), indicating the suitability of M−6 %P-CDs as a “turn-off” fluorescent probe for Cr(VI) detection. Subsequently, we tested the ability of M−6 %P-CDs to detect Cr(VI) in real samples, and the Cr(VI) concentration was detected in tap water, lake water and honey water, respectively, and the results are shown in Table 3 . The M−6 %P-CDs were able to achieve effective detection of Cr(VI) at different concentrations with recoveries and relative standard deviations (RSDs) of 94–105 and 0.68–3.62 %, respectively. These analytical results demonstrated that the M−6 %P-CDs had great potential for application in the detection of real samples. The pH of real industrial effluents typically deviates from neutrality, making it crucial to assess the response of CDs to Cr(VI) in various pH environments. However, this important aspect is often overlooked despite its significant impact on practical applications. Fig. 5d demonstrates that the acid-base environment of the solution does not affect the ability of M−6 %P-CDs to detect Cr(VI), which is attractive in practical applications.

To deeply investigate the quenching mechanism of Cr(VI) on M−6 %P-CDs, the absorption spectra and the changes of fluorescence decay plots of M−6 %P-CDs before and after the presence of Cr(VI) were tested (Fig. 5e and f). As shown in Fig. 5e, the absorption peaks at 286 and 358 nm were enhanced after mixing the CDs with the solution of Cr(VI). Additionally, the lifetime decay plots of the CDs were not significantly different from those of the previous ones (Fig. 5f), indicating that the response mechanism of the CDs to Cr(VI) is static quenching [30]. This speculation was subsequently verified by calculations. According to the Stern-Volmer equation ((I0-I)/I0 = KSV * CCr(VI)), the KSV (quenching constant) for Cr(VI) at the M−6 %P-CDs was 5.1 × 103 mol, and according to the equation KSV = kqτ0, it was known that Kq (quenching rate constant) was 4.47 × 1011 L mol-1s−1, which was greater than the dynamic quenching constant (2 × 1010 L mol-1s−1) [64]. All the above analyses indicate that the quenching mechanism of M−6 %P-CDs with Cr(VI) was static bursting. Fig. 5g visualizes the effect of M−6 %P-CDs on Cr(VI) bursting. When the concentration of Cr(VI) reached 120 μM, it was difficult to be observed by the naked eye.

The photostability of CDs is an often overlooked but very important property. Fig. 6 shows the photostability of M−CDs and M−6 %P-CDs in solution state (Fig. 6a–c) and in PVA films (Fig. 6d–f). It revealed that the continuous irradiation of UV light does not change the emission wavelength of the CDs either dispersed in solution or immobilized in the PVA film, proving that no aggregates or new fluorophores are generated [65]. M−6 %P-CDs show enhanced photostability compared to M−CDs, with a 6 % increase in PL intensity after 70 min of UV irradiation. This is even more pronounced in the PVA films, where the PL intensities of the M−CDs and M−6 %P-CDs are 78.6 and 107.8 % of the initial values, respectively. The film stability of M−6 %P-CDs is improved by 37 %. This is surprising because, in general, the enhancement of photostability requires the sacrifice of PLQYs. The reasons for the observed phenomenon can be summarized as three points based on previous analysis: firstly, the main luminescent states of the M−6 %P-CDs are the carbon core and surface states, and the molecular state accounts for a small percentage (B3 = 1.1 %), thus attenuating the photobleaching phenomenon and improving the stability of the CDs. Secondly, the tight binding of hydrogen bonds in PVA to the surface functional groups of M−6 %P-CDs under UV conditions can enhance the surface passivation of CDs in a restricted environment [66]. In addition, since M−6 %P-CDs cleaves the sp2 structural domains in the lattice structure of CDs, it reduces the preferential photobleaching due to excitation energy migration, which is prone to occur in long conjugated structures [67].

Green-emitting CDs M−6 %P-CDs with a PLQY of 73.9 % were prepared by a one-step solvothermal method using (NH4)2HPO4 as the phosphorus source and MPD and TA as the carbon sources through an improved synthesis process. The PLQY of M−6 %P-CDs was enhanced by a factor of 1.34 as compared to that of the phosphorus-undoped M−CDs. P-doping enabled the CDs to retain more functional groups on their surface and enhanced its alkalinity sensitivity, enabling the M−6 %P-CDs to be used as acid-base indicators with a decrease in pKa value from 10.29 to 8.32. The M−6 %P-CDs showed specific recognition of Cr (VI) with a linear response in the range of 1–130 μM and a limit of detection of 0.59 μM. The stability of M−6 %P-CDs in solution and in PVA matrix was found to be enhanced by 6 % and 37 %, respectively, compared to that of M−CDs. Through characterization and controlled experiments, we propose that P-doped CDs inhibit the non-radiative leaps caused by π-π stacking of carbon nanosheets and reduce the preferential photobleaching phenomenon of the long-conjugated structures by increasing the separability of the sp2 structural domains of the carbon core. The present work provides a reference for the preparation of highly stable and high PLQYs CDs.

Yi Tian: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Yunlong Yue: Supervision, Resources, Conceptualization.
